deconstruction_targets: patrons of archer-wizards (temporal and spiritual Lords)
constructive_proposals: Allow no midwife to practise without being sworn as a good Catholic
Observe safeguards mentioned in the Second Part of this work
Excommunicate patrons and receivers of heretics
Condemn those who continue obstinate in excommunication for a year as heretics

scapegoat.name: archer-wizards
scapegoat.justification: They are condemned as heretics and heresiarchs, receive protection from nobles and princes, and are described as the most cruel enemies of the Christian religion. Their patrons are considered more damnable than they are.

moral_justification: The text frames healing by witches as illegitimate due to their sinful lives, and positions all support—whether through patronage, protection, omission, or commission—of such figures as morally culpable. It justifies intervention through excommunication and legal condemnation of those who enable heresy, grounding morality in religious orthodoxy and duty to the Church.
summary: The passage deconstructs the legitimacy of witch healers by linking their healing power to sinfulness rather than sanctity, then identifies multiple layers of complicity—including midwives, patrons, protectors, receivers, and obstructors—as morally culpable. It proposes legal and religious sanctions such as oaths for midwives, excommunication, and condemnation of persistent enablers, all framed within a rigid Catholic moral and legal framework.
